1:
"And it came to pass in those days, when there was no king in Israel, that there was a certain Levite... who took to him a concubine out of Bethlehemjudah." The witnesses note again that there was no king in Israel, and a Levite who took a concubine. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same is observed before, referring to the times before the judges. The witnesses note that a Levite took a concubine out of Bethlehem-judah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no king in Israel — the refrain; the a certain Levite — of the holy tribe; the a concubine — an irregular union; the every man did right in his eyes — the moral collapse (Judg. 21:25).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens once more with the refrain that frames the whole sad appendix — "in those days, when there was no king in Israel": the want of godly rule, here repeated, accounts for the disorder and the dreadful evil to follow; even a Levite, of the tribe set apart for God, was found in an irregular union; the moral declension that touched even the ministers shows how far Israel had fallen; it teaches that when men cast off the rule of God, corruption spreads through every order, and that the disorders of a people begin in the forsaking of his law, "in those days... every man did that which was right in his own eyes" (Judg. 21:25), the Levite taking a concubine in lawless times.

So the witnesses make the verse no king in Israel: The witnesses note the no king in Israel: the refrain. The witnesses note the a certain Levite: of the holy tribe. The witnesses note the a concubine: an irregular union. The witnesses note the every man did right in his eyes: the moral collapse (Judg. 21:25). The expositors observe the no king in Israel: the refrain. The expositors observe the a certain Levite: of the holy tribe. The expositors observe the a concubine: an irregular union. The expositors observe the every man did right in his eyes: the moral collapse (Judg. 21:25). The believer is taught to mark the no king in Israel, the a certain Levite, the a concubine, the every man did right in his eyes, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that, with no king in Israel, even a Levite took a concubine — the want of godly rule breeding disorder through every order, corruption spreading when men cast off God's law, every man doing what was right in his own eyes (Judg. 21:25).
2:
"And his concubine played the whore against him, and went away from him unto her father's house to Bethlehemjudah, and was there four whole months." The witnesses note the concubine's unfaithfulness and her departure to her father's house. The witnesses note that Gill notes she was unfaithful to him, breaking the covenant between them, or it may mean she was angry and left him. The witnesses note that she was there four whole months. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the played the whore — the unfaithfulness; the went away from him — the breach; the her father's house — four months; the the fruit of disorder — they have dealt treacherously (Hos. 5:7).

The witnesses agree the union was marred by unfaithfulness and breach — "his concubine played the whore against him, and went away from him": whether by infidelity or by some falling-out, she forsook him and returned to her father; the broken and troubled relationship shows the fruit of an alliance not according to God's order; it teaches that unions formed apart from the right rule are prone to breach and sorrow, and that sin within the household brings division, "they have dealt treacherously against the LORD... now shall a month devour them" (Hos. 5:7), the concubine departing to her father's house.

So the witnesses make the verse played the whore: The witnesses note the played the whore: the unfaithfulness. The witnesses note the went away from him: the breach. The witnesses note the her father's house: four months. The witnesses note the the fruit of disorder: they have dealt treacherously (Hos. 5:7). The expositors observe the played the whore: the unfaithfulness. The expositors observe the went away from him: the breach. The expositors observe the her father's house: four months. The expositors observe the the fruit of disorder: they have dealt treacherously (Hos. 5:7). The believer is taught to mark the played the whore, the went away from him, the her father's house, the the fruit of disorder, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the concubine was unfaithful and departed — the fruit of an alliance not according to God's order, unions apart from his rule prone to breach and sorrow, dealing treacherously (Hos. 5:7).
3:
"And her husband arose, and went after her, to speak friendly unto her, and to bring her again... and she brought him into her father's house: and when the father of the damsel saw him, he rejoiced to meet him." The witnesses note the Levite going after his concubine to speak kindly and be reconciled, and the father's glad welcome. The witnesses note that Gill notes he arose from the place where he lived to go after her. The witnesses note that Clarke notes he rejoiced, hoping to reconcile his daughter and her husband. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went after her — to seek her; the to speak friendly — to her heart; the to bring her again — reconciliation; the the father rejoiced — to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10).

The witnesses agree the Levite sought reconciliation rather than revenge — "went after her, to speak friendly unto her, and to bring her again": he came not to upbraid but "to speak to her heart," seeking to win her back in kindness; the father rejoiced, hoping for peace between them; the seeking of the estranged with gentle words shows the way of grace that pursues to restore; it teaches that the wronged may seek reconciliation with the offender, going after the wanderer to bring her home, a faint shadow of the love that "came to seek and to save that which was lost" (Luke 19:10), the Levite going to bring his concubine again.

So the witnesses make the verse to speak friendly: The witnesses note the went after her: to seek her. The witnesses note the to speak friendly: to her heart. The witnesses note the to bring her again: reconciliation. The witnesses note the the father rejoiced: to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10). The expositors observe the went after her: to seek her. The expositors observe the to speak friendly: to her heart. The expositors observe the to bring her again: reconciliation. The expositors observe the the father rejoiced: to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10). The believer is taught to mark the went after her, the to speak friendly, the to bring her again, the the father rejoiced, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the Levite went after his concubine to speak kindly and bring her back — the way of grace that pursues to restore, seeking the estranged with gentle words, a shadow of the love that came to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10).
4:
"And his father in law, the damsel's father, retained him; and he abode with him three days: so they did eat and drink, and lodged there." The witnesses note the father-in-law keeping the Levite three days with feasting and lodging. The witnesses note that Gill notes he prevailed upon him to stay some time with him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him abiding three days, eating, drinking, and lodging. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the father retained him — three days; the did eat and drink — feasting; the lodged there — hospitality; the given to hospitality — given to hospitality (Rom. 12:13).

The witnesses agree the father showed warm and generous hospitality — "his father in law... retained him; and he abode with him three days... did eat and drink, and lodged there": he kept his son-in-law with feasting and lodging, glad of the reconciliation; the open and kindly entertainment shows the grace of hospitality, a virtue commended in Scripture; it teaches that the people of God are to be given to hospitality, receiving and refreshing one another, "given to hospitality" (Rom. 12:13); yet, as the next verses show, even a good thing pressed too far may delay one into danger, the father retaining the Levite three days.

So the witnesses make the verse retained him: The witnesses note the the father retained him: three days. The witnesses note the did eat and drink: feasting. The witnesses note the lodged there: hospitality. The witnesses note the given to hospitality: given to hospitality (Rom. 12:13). The expositors observe the the father retained him: three days. The expositors observe the did eat and drink: feasting. The expositors observe the lodged there: hospitality. The expositors observe the given to hospitality: given to hospitality (Rom. 12:13). The believer is taught to mark the the father retained him, the did eat and drink, the lodged there, the given to hospitality, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the father kept the Levite three days with feasting — the grace of hospitality, the people of God to receive and refresh one another, given to hospitality (Rom. 12:13), yet a good thing pressed too far may delay into danger.
5:
"And it came to pass on the fourth day, when they arose early in the morning... the damsel's father said unto his son in law, Comfort thine heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward go your way." The witnesses note the father urging the Levite to refresh himself before departing on the fourth day. The witnesses note that Gill notes the time the Levite had agreed to stay being up. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes most journeys were performed in the early morning. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the on the fourth day — they arose; the comfort thine heart — with bread; the afterward go — the urging; the redeem the time — redeeming the time, the days are evil (Eph. 5:16).

The witnesses agree the father, loath to part, urged a meal before the journey — "Comfort thine heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward go your way": his pressing of food was kindness, but it began the delays that would keep the Levite till a perilous hour; the urging to "comfort the heart" with bread shows the homely care of hospitality, yet also the danger of letting good cheer delay needful business; it teaches that the people of God must use even lawful comforts with discretion, lest ease and delay lead them into trouble, "redeeming the time, because the days are evil" (Eph. 5:16), the father urging the Levite to tarry for a meal.

So the witnesses make the verse comfort thine heart: The witnesses note the on the fourth day: they arose. The witnesses note the comfort thine heart: with bread. The witnesses note the afterward go: the urging. The witnesses note the redeem the time: redeeming the time, the days are evil (Eph. 5:16). The expositors observe the on the fourth day: they arose. The expositors observe the comfort thine heart: with bread. The expositors observe the afterward go: the urging. The expositors observe the redeem the time: redeeming the time, the days are evil (Eph. 5:16). The believer is taught to mark the on the fourth day, the comfort thine heart, the afterward go, the redeem the time, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the father urged a meal before the journey — kindness that began the delays, the danger of letting good cheer delay needful business, the people of God redeeming the time because the days are evil (Eph. 5:16).
6:
"And they sat down, and did eat and drink both of them together: for the damsel's father had said unto the man, Be content, I pray thee, and tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry." The witnesses note the father pressing the Levite to stay yet another night and be merry. The witnesses note that Gill notes they sat down at table, the Levite having risen to go. The witnesses note that the father said, tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sat down to eat — again; the be content — and tarry; the let thine heart be merry — the urging; the mirth soberly — the laughter of the fool (Eccl. 7:6).

The witnesses agree the father pressed the Levite to stay another night — "tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry": his urging, though kindly meant, again delayed the departure; the repeated pressing to feast and lodge shows how excess of hospitality may keep a man from his duty and his way; it teaches that the people of God should not be so given to ease and merriment as to neglect the time and the road before them, but use mirth soberly, "as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool" (Eccl. 7:6) — yet the heart made merry here delayed the traveller, the father bidding him tarry and be merry.

So the witnesses make the verse tarry and be merry: The witnesses note the sat down to eat: again. The witnesses note the be content: and tarry. The witnesses note the let thine heart be merry: the urging. The witnesses note the mirth soberly: the laughter of the fool (Eccl. 7:6). The expositors observe the sat down to eat: again. The expositors observe the be content: and tarry. The expositors observe the let thine heart be merry: the urging. The expositors observe the mirth soberly: the laughter of the fool (Eccl. 7:6). The believer is taught to mark the sat down to eat, the be content, the let thine heart be merry, the mirth soberly, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the father pressed the Levite to stay and be merry — excess of hospitality keeping a man from his way, the people of God using mirth soberly, not as the crackling of thorns, the laughter of the fool (Eccl. 7:6).
7:
"And when the man rose up to depart, his father in law urged him: therefore he lodged there again." The witnesses note the Levite again rising to leave, but urged by the father to lodge once more. The witnesses note that Gill notes he rose up from table to depart and proceed on his journey. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes his father-in-law urged him to stay. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose up to depart — his resolve; the his father urged him — the pressing; the he lodged there again — overborne; the be stedfast — be ye stedfast, unmoveable (1 Cor. 15:58).

The witnesses agree the Levite's resolve to leave was again overborne — "when the man rose up to depart, his father in law urged him: therefore he lodged there again": his good intention to be gone was set aside by the pressing of his host; the yielding to importunity, though in a small matter, shows how a man's purpose may be turned by the urging of others; it teaches that the people of God should hold to their right resolutions and not be lightly moved from their course, lest the well-meant pressure of friends lead them from their way, "be ye stedfast, unmoveable" (1 Cor. 15:58), the Levite lodging again at his father-in-law's urging.

So the witnesses make the verse lodged again: The witnesses note the rose up to depart: his resolve. The witnesses note the his father urged him: the pressing. The witnesses note the he lodged there again: overborne. The witnesses note the be stedfast: be ye stedfast, unmoveable (1 Cor. 15:58). The expositors observe the rose up to depart: his resolve. The expositors observe the his father urged him: the pressing. The expositors observe the he lodged there again: overborne. The expositors observe the be stedfast: be ye stedfast, unmoveable (1 Cor. 15:58). The believer is taught to mark the rose up to depart, the his father urged him, the he lodged there again, the be stedfast, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the Levite's resolve to leave was overborne and he lodged again — a man's purpose turned by the urging of others, the people of God to hold to right resolutions, be ye stedfast, unmoveable (1 Cor. 15:58).
8:
"And he arose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart: and the damsel's father said, Comfort thine heart. And they tarried until afternoon, and they did eat both of them." The witnesses note the fifth day's delay, the Levite tarrying until afternoon at the father's urging. The witnesses note that Gill notes he rose with a full intention to take his leave and be gone. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them tarrying until afternoon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fifth day — to depart; the comfort thine heart — again; the tarried until afternoon — the day far spent; the walk in the light — walk while ye have the light (John 12:35).

The witnesses agree the delays mounted, the Levite kept till the day was far spent — "the damsel's father said, Comfort thine heart... they tarried until afternoon": the repeated urging consumed the morning, so that the journey, when at last begun, fell in a dangerous hour; the heaping-up of delay shows how a course of small yieldings may bring a man into peril he might have escaped; it teaches that the loss of time and the procrastination of needful action open the door to trouble, and that the wise improve the day while it is theirs, "walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you" (John 12:35), the Levite tarrying till afternoon.

So the witnesses make the verse tarried until afternoon: The witnesses note the the fifth day: to depart. The witnesses note the comfort thine heart: again. The witnesses note the tarried until afternoon: the day far spent. The witnesses note the walk in the light: walk while ye have the light (John 12:35). The expositors observe the the fifth day: to depart. The expositors observe the comfort thine heart: again. The expositors observe the tarried until afternoon: the day far spent. The expositors observe the walk in the light: walk while ye have the light (John 12:35). The believer is taught to mark the the fifth day, the comfort thine heart, the tarried until afternoon, the walk in the light, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the Levite tarried until afternoon at the father's urging — a course of small yieldings bringing peril, the loss of time opening the door to trouble, walk while ye have the light lest darkness come upon you (John 12:35).
9:
"And when the man rose up to depart, he, and his concubine, and his servant... the damsel's father said, Behold, now the day draweth toward evening, I pray you tarry all night..." The witnesses note the father urging the Levite to stay yet again, the day drawing toward evening. The witnesses note that Gill notes they rose up, having refreshed themselves comfortably. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the father urging them to tarry, the day declining. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose up to depart — at last; the the day draweth toward evening — late; the tarry all night — the urging; the look well to his going — the prudent looketh well to his going (Prov. 14:15).

The witnesses agree the father pressed one more night, the day now far gone — "the day draweth toward evening, I pray you tarry all night": his counsel, this time, was not without reason, for the hour was late; yet the Levite, weary of the delays, would go; the lateness of the hour, the fruit of all the former tarrying, now set him on the road at a perilous time; it teaches how the accumulated delays of ease may force a man to travel in danger, and that the people of God must weigh the time and not let pleasure rob them of prudence, "the prudent man looketh well to his going" (Prov. 14:15), the father urging the Levite to tarry the night.

So the witnesses make the verse the day toward evening: The witnesses note the rose up to depart: at last. The witnesses note the the day draweth toward evening: late. The witnesses note the tarry all night: the urging. The witnesses note the look well to his going: the prudent looketh well to his going (Prov. 14:15). The expositors observe the rose up to depart: at last. The expositors observe the the day draweth toward evening: late. The expositors observe the tarry all night: the urging. The expositors observe the look well to his going: the prudent looketh well to his going (Prov. 14:15). The believer is taught to mark the rose up to depart, the the day draweth toward evening, the tarry all night, the look well to his going, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the father urged the Levite to tarry, the day far gone — the accumulated delays of ease forcing travel at a perilous time, the people of God to weigh the time, the prudent looking well to his going (Prov. 14:15).
10:
"But the man would not tarry that night, but he rose up and departed, and came over against Jebus, which is Jerusalem; and there were with him two asses saddled, his concubine also was with him." The witnesses note the Levite refusing to stay, departing late, and coming near Jebus. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was determined on his journey, seeing no end to the solicitations. The witnesses note that he came over against Jebus, which is Jerusalem. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the would not tarry — he departed; the the late hour — night near; the over against Jebus — which is Jerusalem; the a season for all — to every thing there is a season (Eccl. 3:1).

The witnesses agree the Levite, weary of delay, set out though the hour was late — "the man would not tarry that night, but... departed, and came over against Jebus": at last he broke away, but so late that night would overtake him on the road; the coming near to Jebus (the Jebusite stronghold, not yet Israel's) set before him the choice of lodging that would lead to the dreadful night at Gibeah; the departing at an ill hour shows how the consequences of delay press upon a man; it teaches that the wise order their goings in season, and that the people of God must not be ruled by others' importunity to their hurt, "to every thing there is a season" (Eccl. 3:1), the Levite departing late toward Jebus.

So the witnesses make the verse departed toward Jebus: The witnesses note the would not tarry: he departed. The witnesses note the the late hour: night near. The witnesses note the over against Jebus: which is Jerusalem. The witnesses note the a season for all: to every thing there is a season (Eccl. 3:1). The expositors observe the would not tarry: he departed. The expositors observe the the late hour: night near. The expositors observe the over against Jebus: which is Jerusalem. The expositors observe the a season for all: to every thing there is a season (Eccl. 3:1). The believer is taught to mark the would not tarry, the the late hour, the over against Jebus, the a season for all, and to mourn the moral ruin that follows the forsaking of God. The verse thus records that the Levite departed late and came near Jebus — the consequences of delay pressing upon him, the wise ordering their goings in season, to every thing there is a season (Eccl. 3:1).
11:
"And when they were by Jebus, the day was far spent; and the servant said unto his master, Come, I pray thee, and let us turn in into this city of the Jebusites, and lodge in it." The witnesses note the day far spent by Jebus, and the servant proposing to lodge there. The witnesses note that Gill notes the sun was going down apace and near setting. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Jebus was Jerusalem, then held by the Jebusites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the by Jebus — the day far spent; the the servant said — let us turn in; the the city of the Jebusites — the heathen town; the the night cometh — the night cometh, when no man can work (John 9:4).

The witnesses agree the lateness of the hour pressed them to seek lodging — "the day was far spent; and the servant said... let us turn in into this city of the Jebusites": the servant, seeing night fall, would have them shelter in the nearest town, though it was a city of the heathen; the urgency of the failing light shows the strait the delay had brought them to; it teaches that the loss of the day leaves a man pressed for shelter in unfit places, and that the people of God do well to provide in season against the night, "the night cometh, when no man can work" (John 9:4), the day far spent by Jebus.

So the witnesses make the verse the day far spent: The witnesses note the by Jebus: the day far spent. The witnesses note the the servant said: let us turn in. The witnesses note the the city of the Jebusites: the heathen town. The witnesses note the the night cometh: the night cometh, when no man can work (John 9:4). The believer is taught to mark the by Jebus, the the servant said, the the city of the Jebusites, the the night cometh, and to entertain strangers and to fear the moral ruin of a godless people. The verse thus records that the day was far spent by Jebus and the servant urged lodging there — the strait the delay had brought, the people of God to provide in season against the night, the night cometh when no man can work (John 9:4).
12:
"And his master said unto him, We will not turn aside hither into the city of a stranger, that is not of the children of Israel; we will pass over to Gibeah." The witnesses note the Levite refusing the Gentile city, choosing to lodge among his own people at Gibeah. The witnesses note that Gill notes he would not turn into a city of the Gentiles, the Jebusites being of the seven nations. The witnesses note that he chose to pass over to Gibeah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not the city of a stranger — not of Israel; the we will pass over — to Gibeah; the safety among his own — expected; the Israel below the heathen — fornication not named among the Gentiles (1 Cor. 5:1).

The witnesses agree the Levite chose an Israelite town, expecting safety among his own — "We will not turn aside... into the city of a stranger... we will pass over to Gibeah": he would not trust himself among the heathen, but looked for kindness from his brethren in Israel; the choice, reasonable in itself, met a bitter irony, for he found worse evil among God's people than he feared among the heathen; it teaches that the corruption of a professing people may sink below that of the heathen, and that the name of Israel was no warrant of righteousness where the fear of God was cast off, "it is reported commonly... such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles" (1 Cor. 5:1), the Levite choosing Gibeah over the stranger's city.

So the witnesses make the verse not the city of a stranger: The witnesses note the not the city of a stranger: not of Israel. The witnesses note the we will pass over: to Gibeah. The witnesses note the safety among his own: expected. The witnesses note the Israel below the heathen: fornication not named among the Gentiles (1 Cor. 5:1). The expositors observe the not the city of a stranger: not of Israel. The expositors observe the we will pass over: to Gibeah. The expositors observe the safety among his own: expected. The expositors observe the Israel below the heathen: fornication not named among the Gentiles (1 Cor. 5:1). The believer is taught to mark the not the city of a stranger, the we will pass over, the safety among his own, the Israel below the heathen, and to entertain strangers and to fear the moral ruin of a godless people. The verse thus records that the Levite chose Gibeah over the Gentile city, expecting safety among his own — the bitter irony that Israel's corruption sank below the heathen's, the name of Israel no warrant of righteousness, sin not so much as named among the Gentiles (1 Cor. 5:1).
13:
"And he said unto his servant, Come, and let us draw near to one of these places to lodge all night, in Gibeah, or in Ramah." The witnesses note the Levite resolving to lodge at Gibeah or Ramah. The witnesses note that Gill notes he urged them to get on as fast as they could. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Ramah was only two miles beyond Gibeah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let us draw near — to lodge; the in Gibeah — or in Ramah; the among his own — the safer way; the the LORD directs the steps — the LORD directeth his steps (Prov. 16:9).

The witnesses agree the Levite pressed on to reach a lodging among his own — "let us draw near to one of these places to lodge... in Gibeah, or in Ramah": he hastened toward the towns of Benjamin, intending to rest the night there; the resolve to lodge among the people of God seemed the safer way; yet his very seeking of shelter among Israel would bring him to the scene of a great wickedness; it teaches that man purposes his way but the issue is in God's hand, and that even prudent choices may lead, by providence, into trial, "a man's heart deviseth his way: but the LORD directeth his steps" (Prov. 16:9), the Levite drawing near to Gibeah or Ramah.

So the witnesses make the verse Gibeah or Ramah: The witnesses note the let us draw near: to lodge. The witnesses note the in Gibeah: or in Ramah. The witnesses note the among his own: the safer way. The witnesses note the the LORD directs the steps: the LORD directeth his steps (Prov. 16:9). The expositors observe the let us draw near: to lodge. The expositors observe the in Gibeah: or in Ramah. The expositors observe the among his own: the safer way. The expositors observe the the LORD directs the steps: the LORD directeth his steps (Prov. 16:9). The believer is taught to mark the let us draw near, the in Gibeah, the among his own, the the LORD directs the steps, and to entertain strangers and to fear the moral ruin of a godless people. The verse thus records that the Levite resolved to lodge at Gibeah or Ramah — the seeming-safer way among his own, yet leading by providence into trial, man devising his way but the LORD directing his steps (Prov. 16:9).
14:
"And they passed on and went their way; and the sun went down upon them when they were by Gibeah, which belongeth to Benjamin." The witnesses note the sun setting upon them as they came to Gibeah of Benjamin. The witnesses note that Gill notes they proceeded on their journey from near Jerusalem. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes they were evidently reluctant, the sun going down upon them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the passed on — their way; the the sun went down — upon them; the by Gibeah — of Benjamin; the know not the morrow — ye know not what shall be on the morrow (James 4:14).

The witnesses agree night overtook them at Gibeah — "the sun went down upon them when they were by Gibeah, which belongeth to Benjamin": the darkness fell as they reached the town of Benjamin, fixing their lodging there; the closing-in of night, the fruit of all the day's delay, shut them up to Gibeah; it teaches how the consequences of lost time gather to a point, and that the providence of God brings men to the very place appointed for their trial, the darkness folding them into Gibeah where the evil lay, "ye know not what shall be on the morrow" (James 4:14), the sun going down upon them by Gibeah.

So the witnesses make the verse the sun went down: The witnesses note the passed on: their way. The witnesses note the the sun went down: upon them. The witnesses note the by Gibeah: of Benjamin. The witnesses note the know not the morrow: ye know not what shall be on the morrow (James 4:14). The expositors observe the passed on: their way. The expositors observe the the sun went down: upon them. The expositors observe the by Gibeah: of Benjamin. The expositors observe the know not the morrow: ye know not what shall be on the morrow (James 4:14). The believer is taught to mark the passed on, the the sun went down, the by Gibeah, the know not the morrow, and to entertain strangers and to fear the moral ruin of a godless people. The verse thus records that the sun set upon them at Gibeah of Benjamin — the consequences of lost time gathering to a point, providence bringing men to the place appointed for their trial, knowing not what shall be on the morrow (James 4:14).
15:
"And they turned aside thither, to go in and to lodge in Gibeah: and when he went in, he sat him down in a street of the city: for there was no man that took them into his house to lodging." The witnesses note the travellers sitting in the street of Gibeah, for none took them in. The witnesses note that Gill notes they turned out of their road into the city to seek a lodging. The witnesses note that no man took them into his house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turned aside — to lodge; the sat in the street — of the city; the no man took them in — the cold neglect; the the stranger unwelcomed — i was a stranger, and ye took me not in (Matt. 25:43).

The witnesses agree Gibeah showed a cold and shameful inhospitality — "he sat him down in a street of the city: for there was no man that took them into his house": though strangers and travellers, none of the townsmen would receive them; the failure to show the common kindness of shelter exposes the hardness and decay of Gibeah; it teaches that the want of hospitality is a mark of a people fallen from love and the fear of God, and that the cold neglect of the stranger is itself a sin, "I was a stranger, and ye took me not in" (Matt. 25:43), the travellers left in the street of Gibeah.

So the witnesses make the verse no man took them in: The witnesses note the turned aside: to lodge. The witnesses note the sat in the street: of the city. The witnesses note the no man took them in: the cold neglect. The witnesses note the the stranger unwelcomed: I was a stranger, and ye took me not in (Matt. 25:43). 16:
"And, behold, there came an old man from his work out of the field at even, which was also of mount Ephraim; and he sojourned in Gibeah: but the men of the place were Benjamites." The witnesses note an old man, a sojourner from Ephraim, coming from his work, while the townsmen were Benjamites. The witnesses note that Gill notes he came from his labour at even, the time men take their rest. The witnesses note that he was of mount Ephraim, sojourning in Gibeah, but the men of the place were Benjamites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an old man — from his work; the of mount Ephraim — a sojourner; the the men were Benjamites — the natives; the grace in the stranger — a faithful man who can find? (Prov. 20:6).

The witnesses agree the one who showed kindness was a stranger, not a native of Gibeah — "an old man... was also of mount Ephraim; and he sojourned in Gibeah: but the men of the place were Benjamites": the diligent old man, come from his honest labour, was himself but a sojourner, while the true-born of Gibeah showed no mercy; the marking that the kind man was a foreigner and the cruel ones the natives shows how grace may dwell in the stranger while the children of the place are corrupt; it teaches that righteousness is not bound to birth or place, and that God preserves a remnant of mercy even in a wicked town, the old man come from his field, "a faithful man who can find?" (Prov. 20:6), the old sojourner from Ephraim.

(Prov. 20:6).
17:
"And when he had lifted up his eyes, he saw a wayfaring man in the street of the city: and the old man said, Whither goest thou? and whence comest thou?" The witnesses note the old man seeing the stranger and kindly inquiring after him. The witnesses note that Gill notes coming into the city he looked up and saw the man. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him asking, Whither goest thou? whence comest thou? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the lifted up his eyes — saw the wayfarer; the a wayfaring man — in the street; the whither goest thou — his kind inquiry; the an eye for the needy — look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4).

The witnesses agree the old man, unlike the rest, noticed and cared for the stranger — "the old man said, Whither goest thou? and whence comest thou?": he alone took heed of the wayfarer sitting forlorn in the street, and inquired with kindness; the turning of his eye and heart toward the neglected traveller shows the compassion that marks the godly; it teaches that the people of God are to have an eye for the stranger and the needy whom others pass by, and to inquire and care for them, "look not every man on his own things, but every man also on the things of others" (Phil. 2:4), the old man inquiring after the wayfarer.

So the witnesses make the verse whither goest thou: The witnesses note the lifted up his eyes: saw the wayfarer. The witnesses note the a wayfaring man: in the street. The witnesses note the whither goest thou: his kind inquiry. The witnesses note the an eye for the needy: look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4). 18:
"And he said unto him, We are passing from Bethlehemjudah toward the side of mount Ephraim... and I am now going to the house of the LORD; and there is no man that receiveth me to house." The witnesses note the Levite telling his way, his purpose for the house of the LORD, and that none received him. The witnesses note that Gill notes he answers the last question first, telling whence he came. The witnesses note that he said, I am going to the house of the LORD, and no man receiveth me. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the passing from Bethlehem — to Ephraim; the going to the house of the LORD — his errand; the no man receiveth me — the neglect; the none cared for the soul — no man cared for my soul (Ps. 142:4).

The witnesses agree the Levite named his sacred errand and his sad neglect — "I am now going to the house of the LORD; and there is no man that receiveth me to house": he served at God's house, yet found no welcome among God's people; the complaint that none received a servant of the sanctuary lays bare how low the love of God and his ministers had fallen; it teaches that a people grown cold to God grow cold to his servants and to the stranger, and that the neglect of those who go about the LORD's work is a sign of decayed religion, "no man cared for my soul" (Ps. 142:4), the Levite finding none to receive him.

So the witnesses make the verse the house of the LORD: The witnesses note the passing from Bethlehem: to Ephraim. The witnesses note the going to the house of the LORD: his errand. 19:
"Yet there is both straw and provender for our asses; and there is bread and wine also for me... there is no want of any thing." The witnesses note the Levite assuring the old man he needed nothing but shelter, having his own provision. The witnesses note that Gill notes he had straw to litter the asses and provender to feed them, brought with him. The witnesses note that Clarke notes there was bread and provender, lacking nothing but lodging. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the straw and provender — for the asses; the bread and wine — for himself; the no want of any thing — only shelter; the content with little — having food and raiment, be content (1 Tim. 6:8).

The witnesses agree the Levite sought only a roof, not to be a burden — "there is both straw and provender... bread and wine also... there is no want of any thing": he would lodge at no man's cost, having brought his own supply, asking only shelter; the modesty that would not burden a host shows a becoming consideration in the one seeking kindness; it teaches that the people of God, even in their need, should be considerate and not grasping, content with little and unwilling to burden others, "having food and raiment let us be therewith content" (1 Tim. 6:8), the Levite needing only shelter.

So the witnesses make the verse no want of any thing: The witnesses note the straw and provender: for the asses. The witnesses note the bread and wine: for himself. 20:
"And the old man said, Peace be with thee; howsoever let all thy wants lie upon me; only lodge not in the street." The witnesses note the old man's generous welcome, taking all the stranger's wants upon himself. The witnesses note that Gill notes he wished him peace and tranquillity of mind, let nothing distress him. The witnesses note that Clarke notes all thy wants lie upon me, the old man taking the charge. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the peace be with thee — his welcome; the let all thy wants lie on me — the charge; the lodge not in the street — received; the entertain strangers — some have entertained angels unawares (Heb. 13:2).

The witnesses agree the old man received the stranger with full and free hospitality — "Peace be with thee... let all thy wants lie upon me; only lodge not in the street": he would not suffer the traveller to lie out in the open, but took the whole charge of his needs upon himself; the warm and generous welcome shines the brighter against the coldness of Gibeah; it teaches the grace and duty of hospitality, that the people of God should freely receive and provide for the stranger, "be not forgetful to entertain strangers: for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" (Heb. 13:2), the old man taking the stranger's wants upon himself.

So the witnesses make the verse all thy wants on me: The witnesses note the peace be with thee: his welcome. The witnesses note the let all thy wants lie on me: the charge. 21:
"So he brought him into his house, and gave provender unto the asses: and they washed their feet, and did eat and drink." The witnesses note the old man bringing the travellers in, feeding the asses, and refreshing his guests. The witnesses note that Gill notes he showed him the way and in a kind and friendly manner bid him welcome. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them washing their feet, eating, and drinking. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought him in — to his house; the provender for the asses — provided; the washed their feet — refreshed; the hospitality without grudging — use hospitality without grudging (1 Pet. 4:9).

The witnesses agree the old man's hospitality was full and gracious — "he brought him into his house... they washed their feet, and did eat and drink": he provided for beast and man, and made his guests welcome at his table; the kindly entertainment shines as a last gleam of decency before the dark deed; it teaches that the duty of hospitality is to be done heartily and completely, refreshing the weary stranger, "given to hospitality" and "use hospitality one to another without grudging" (1 Pet. 4:9), the old man entertaining his guests.

22:
"Now as they were making their hearts merry, behold, the men of the city, certain sons of Belial, beset the house round about, and beat at the door..." The witnesses note the wicked men of Gibeah, sons of Belial, beset the house with vile intent. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were making their hearts merry with a glass after supper. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sons of Belial besetting the house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the making their hearts merry — the evening; the the men of the city — sons of Belial; the beset the house — the vile mob; the Israel become as Sodom — they declare their sin as Sodom (Isa. 3:9).

The witnesses agree the peace of the evening was shattered by a mob of the basest men — "the men of the city, certain sons of Belial, beset the house round about": their vile demand, like the men of Sodom of old, revealed a wickedness within Israel as deep as the heathen's worst; the besetting of the house by "sons of Belial" shows how far Gibeah had sunk, repeating the very sin of Sodom (Gen. 19:4-5); it teaches that where the fear of God is cast off, men give themselves to the foulest sins, and that a professing people may become as Sodom, "they declare their sin as Sodom, they hide it not" (Isa. 3:9), the sons of Belial besetting the house.

So the witnesses make the verse sons of Belial: The witnesses note the making their hearts merry: the evening. The witnesses note the the men of the city: sons of Belial. The verse thus records that the sons of Belial beset the house with a vile demand like Sodom's — a wickedness within Israel as deep as the heathen's worst, a professing people become as Sodom where the fear of God is cast off, declaring their sin as Sodom (Isa. 3:9).
23:
"And the man, the master of the house, went out unto them, and said unto them, Nay, my brethren, nay, I pray you, do not so wickedly... do not this folly." The witnesses note the old man going out to plead with the mob not to do so great a wickedness. The witnesses note that Gill notes he went out to converse with them and reason with them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set his plea, do not so wickedly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the master of the house — went out; the do not so wickedly — his plea; the do not this folly — named for what it was; the reprove the works of darkness — reprove the unfruitful works of darkness (Eph. 5:11).

The witnesses agree the old man pleaded earnestly against the evil — "Nay, my brethren... do not so wickedly... do not this folly": he set himself, one righteous man, against the lawless crowd, calling their purpose by its true name, wickedness and folly; the bold remonstrance of the godly against open sin shows the duty to withstand evil and to plead for righteousness even against a multitude; it teaches that the people of God must not be silent before great wickedness, but reprove it plainly, "have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather reprove them" (Eph. 5:11), the old man pleading against the folly.

So the witnesses make the verse do not so wickedly: The witnesses note the the master of the house: went out. The witnesses note the do not so wickedly: his plea. The verse thus records that the old man pleaded against the great wickedness — the duty to withstand and reprove open sin even against a multitude, the people of God not silent before evil, reproving the unfruitful works of darkness (Eph. 5:11).
24:
"Behold, here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out now, and humble ye them... but unto this man do not so vile a thing." The witnesses note the old man's grievous offer of the women to turn the mob from his guest. The witnesses note that Gill notes he offered his own daughter, a virgin, and the concubine of the Levite. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the offer to spare the man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the here is my daughter — a maiden; the and his concubine — offered; the do not so vile a thing — to the man; the no evil for good — let us not do evil that good may come (Rom. 3:8).

The witnesses agree the old man, in his extremity, made a dreadful and sinful offer — "here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out... but unto this man do not so vile a thing": meaning to shield his guest by the laws of hospitality, he proposed to give up the women to evil, as Lot once did at Sodom (Gen. 19:8); yet this was no righteous choice but a grievous fault, the doing of one evil to prevent another, which Scripture records but does not approve; it teaches that we may never do evil that good may come, and that even good men, in fear and confusion, may fall into great sin when they lean to their own wisdom rather than to God, "let us do evil, that good may come? whose damnation is just" (Rom. 3:8), the old man's sinful offer.

So the witnesses make the verse his grievous offer: The witnesses note the here is my daughter: a maiden. The witnesses note the and his concubine: offered. The witnesses note the do not so vile a thing: to the man. The witnesses note the no evil for good: let us not do evil that good may come (Rom. 3:8). 25:
"But the men would not hearken to him: so the man took his concubine, and brought her forth unto them; and they knew her, and abused her all the night until the morning..." The witnesses note the mob refusing the plea, and the cruel outrage done to the concubine through the night. The witnesses note that Gill notes the men would not hearken to him. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the abuse continuing all the night until the morning. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the would not hearken — deaf to the plea; the the man brought her forth — the helpless woman; the abused her all night — the outrage; the sin become violence — their feet are swift to shed blood (Rom. 3:15-17).

The witnesses agree the wicked would not be turned, and a fearful cruelty was done — "the men would not hearken... and they... abused her all the night": deaf to all reason, the sons of Belial wrought their outrage upon the helpless woman through the long night; the unrestrained cruelty of the mob shows the savagery to which sin unbridled descends; it teaches that lust let loose becomes pitiless violence, and that where God is not feared the weak are devoured without mercy, "their feet are swift to shed blood... and the way of peace have they not known" (Rom. 3:15-17), the cruel outrage done at Gibeah.

So the witnesses make the verse the cruel outrage: The witnesses note the would not hearken: deaf to the plea. The witnesses note the the man brought her forth: the helpless woman. The witnesses note the abused her all night: the outrage. The witnesses note the sin become violence: their feet are swift to shed blood (Rom. 3:15-17). 26:
"Then came the woman in the dawning of the day, and fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was, till it was light." The witnesses note the stricken woman crawling back at dawn and falling at the door. The witnesses note that Gill notes the wicked wretches who abused her had left her at dawn, at some distance from the house. The witnesses note that she fell down at the door where her lord was, till it was light. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the in the dawning — of the day; the fell down at the door — of the house; the where her lord was — till it was light; the the LORD hears the poor — the LORD despiseth not his prisoners (Ps. 69:33).

The witnesses agree the suffering woman came back only to die at the threshold — "the woman... fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was, till it was light": broken by the night's cruelty, she dragged herself to the door of her shelter and there sank down; the pitiful sight of the victim fallen at the door is a silent witness against the horror done to her; it teaches the bitterness of suffering wrought by men's sin, and bids us mourn for the innocent who perish under the cruelty of the wicked, "the LORD heareth the poor, and despiseth not his prisoners" (Ps. 69:33), the woman fallen at the door.

So the witnesses make the verse fallen at the door: The witnesses note the in the dawning: of the day. The witnesses note the fell down at the door: of the house. The witnesses note the where her lord was: till it was light. The witnesses note the the LORD hears the poor: the LORD despiseth not his prisoners (Ps. 69:33). The verse thus records that the stricken woman crawled back and fell at the door — the bitterness of suffering wrought by men's sin, mourning for the innocent who perish under the cruelty of the wicked, the LORD hearing the poor and despising not his prisoners (Ps. 69:33).
27:
"And her lord rose up in the morning, and opened the doors of the house, and went out to go his way: and, behold, the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door... and her hands were upon the threshold." The witnesses note the Levite finding his concubine fallen at the door, her hands upon the threshold. The witnesses note that Gill notes it is scarcely reasonable to think he slept quietly after such a riot. The witnesses note that he found her fallen at the door, her hands upon the threshold. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the her lord rose up — in the morning; the opened the doors — to go his way; the fallen at the door — her hands on the threshold; the behold the sorrow — is there any sorrow like my sorrow (Lam. 1:12).

The witnesses agree the morning revealed the dreadful end of the night's cruelty — "the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door... and her hands were upon the threshold": she lay where she had striven to reach the shelter, her hands stretched to the door that was shut against her too late; the mute image of her last effort speaks the whole tragedy; it teaches the fearful fruit of a nation's sin, brought home to one household in grief, and bids the people of God consider how far the forsaking of his law had carried Israel, "is it nothing to you, all ye that pass by? behold, and see if there be any sorrow like unto my sorrow" (Lam. 1:12), the woman fallen with her hands upon the threshold.

So the witnesses make the verse hands upon the threshold: The witnesses note the her lord rose up: in the morning. The witnesses note the opened the doors: to go his way. The witnesses note the fallen at the door: her hands on the threshold. The witnesses note the behold the sorrow: is there any sorrow like my sorrow (Lam. 1:12). 28:
"And he said unto her, Up, and let us be going. But none answered. Then the man took her up upon an ass, and the man rose up, and gat him unto his place." The witnesses note the Levite finding his concubine dead, and carrying her body home. The witnesses note that Gill notes he spoke to her as supposing her asleep, to awake her. The witnesses note that none answered, and he took her up upon the ass. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the up, let us be going — his word; the none answered — she was dead; the took her up — upon the ass; the blood cries for justice — thy brother's blood crieth from the ground (Gen. 4:10).

The witnesses agree the dreadful silence told the Levite she was dead — "Up, and let us be going. But none answered": his word to the supposed sleeper met no reply, for she was gone; the still and lifeless body, lifted upon the ass, bore home the full weight of the tragedy; the carrying of the dead concubine to his place set in motion the reckoning that would follow; it teaches that the wages of sin is death, and that the cruelty of the wicked leaves a trail of grief that cries to heaven for justice, "the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground" (Gen. 4:10), the Levite carrying his concubine home.

So the witnesses make the verse none answered: The witnesses note the up, let us be going: his word. The witnesses note the none answered: she was dead. The witnesses note the took her up: upon the ass. The witnesses note the blood cries for justice: thy brother's blood crieth from the ground (Gen. 4:10). The expositors observe the up, let us be going: his word. The expositors observe the none answered: she was dead. The expositors observe the took her up: upon the ass. The expositors observe the blood cries for justice: thy brother's blood crieth from the ground (Gen. 4:10). The believer is taught to mark the up, let us be going, the none answered, the took her up, the blood cries for justice, and to tremble at the depths of sin where God is forsaken. The verse thus records that the Levite found his concubine dead and bore her home — the wages of sin death, the cruelty of the wicked leaving a trail of grief that cries to heaven, the voice of blood crying from the ground (Gen. 4:10).
29:
"And when he was come into his house, he took a knife, and laid hold on his concubine, and divided her, together with her bones, into twelve pieces, and sent her into all the coasts of Israel." The witnesses note the Levite dividing the body and sending the pieces throughout Israel to rouse the tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes he took the dead body, laid it in order, and divided it. The witnesses note that Clarke notes he divided her and sent the pieces into all the coasts of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took a knife — in his house; the divided her — into twelve pieces; the sent her into all Israel — the summons; the purge out the wicked — put away the wicked person (1 Cor. 5:13).

The witnesses agree the Levite chose a fearful means to stir the conscience of the nation — "he... divided her... into twelve pieces, and sent her into all the coasts of Israel": by this dreadful summons he laid the crime before every tribe, that none might be ignorant or indifferent; the startling deed, however strange, was meant to rouse a slumbering people to take notice of so great an evil; it teaches that great sin in the midst of God's people must not be hidden or ignored, but brought to light that it may be judged, and that the whole body shares the duty to purge out wickedness, "put away from among yourselves that wicked person" (1 Cor. 5:13), the body sent throughout Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse twelve pieces: The witnesses note the took a knife: in his house. The witnesses note the divided her: into twelve pieces. The witnesses note the sent her into all Israel: the summons. The witnesses note the purge out the wicked: put away the wicked person (1 Cor. 5:13). The expositors observe the took a knife: in his house. The expositors observe the divided her: into twelve pieces. The expositors observe the sent her into all Israel: the summons. The expositors observe the purge out the wicked: put away the wicked person (1 Cor. 5:13). The believer is taught to mark the took a knife, the divided her, the sent her into all Israel, the purge out the wicked, and to tremble at the depths of sin where God is forsaken. The verse thus records that the Levite divided the body and sent the pieces throughout Israel to rouse the tribes — great sin among God's people not to be hidden but brought to light and judged, the whole body sharing the duty to purge out wickedness, put away the wicked person (1 Cor. 5:13).
30:
"And it was so, that all that saw it said, There was no such deed done nor seen from the day that the children of Israel came up out of the land of Egypt unto this day: consider of it, take advice, and speak your minds." The witnesses note all Israel shocked at the deed, and the call to consider, take counsel, and act. The witnesses note that Gill notes all that saw the pieces were made acquainted with the cause. The witnesses note that they said, consider of it, take advice, and speak. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all that saw it — were shocked; the no such deed — since Egypt; the consider of it — take advice and speak; the weigh your ways — consider your ways (Hag. 1:7).

The witnesses agree the nation was roused to horror and to counsel — "There was no such deed done... from the day... Israel came out of Egypt... consider... take advice, and speak": the unheard-of wickedness shocked all who saw, and the cry went out to weigh the matter and resolve what to do; the rousing of the people's conscience shows that there is a point at which sin so cries out that none can be silent; it teaches that the people of God must consider gravely the evil among them and take counsel for righteous judgment, not letting great wickedness pass, "consider your ways" (Hag. 1:7); thus the dark chapter closes, the nation called to consider, take advice, and speak.

So the witnesses make the verse consider, take advice: The witnesses note the all that saw it: were shocked. The witnesses note the no such deed: since Egypt. The witnesses note the consider of it: take advice and speak. The witnesses note the weigh your ways: consider your ways (Hag. 1:7). The expositors observe the all that saw it: were shocked. The expositors observe the no such deed: since Egypt. The expositors observe the consider of it: take advice and speak. The expositors observe the weigh your ways: consider your ways (Hag. 1:7). The believer is taught to mark the all that saw it, the no such deed, the consider of it, the weigh your ways, and to tremble at the depths of sin where God is forsaken. The verse thus records that all Israel was shocked and called to consider, take counsel, and act — sin crying out so that none can be silent, the people of God to weigh gravely the evil among them and take counsel for righteous judgment, consider your ways (Hag. 1:7).
